an6.63:1.1: “Bhikkhus, I will teach you a penetrative exposition, an exposition of *dhamma* [*dhammapariyāyaṁ*].
an6.63:1.2: Listen to it, attend to it closely, I will speak.”
an6.63:10.2: Contact, bhikkhus, is the arising from the source of sensual pleasures.
an6.63:11.1: And what, bhikkhus, is the diversity of sensual pleasures?
an6.63:11.2: One kind of sensual pleasure is in forms, another kind of sensual pleasure is in sounds, another kind of sensual pleasure is in smells, another kind of sensual pleasure is in tastes, another kind of sensual pleasure is in tangible things.
an6.63:11.3: This is called the diversity of sensual pleasures, bhikkhus.
an6.63:12.1: And what, bhikkhus, is the result of sensual pleasures?
an6.63:12.2: When, bhikkhus, desiring sensual pleasures, one brings forth this or that embodied existence, either belonging to merit or belonging to demerit, this is called the result of sensual pleasures, bhikkhus.
an6.63:13.1: And what, bhikkhus, is the cessation of sensual pleasures?
an6.63:13.2: The cessation of contact, bhikkhus, is the cessation of sensual pleasures.
an6.63:13.3: This very Noble Eightfold Path is the practice leading to the cessation of sensual pleasures, that is:
an6.63:13.4: right view, right intention, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right *sati*, right *samādhi*.
an6.63:14.1: When, bhikkhus, a noble disciple understands sensual pleasures in this way, understands the arising from the source of sensual pleasures in this way, understands the diversity of sensual pleasures in this way, understands the result of sensual pleasures in this way, understands the cessation of sensual pleasures in this way, understands the practice leading to the cessation of sensual pleasures in this way, that one understands this penetrative holy life as the cessation of sensual pleasures.
an6.63:14.2: Sensual pleasures, bhikkhus, should be understood … the practice leading to the cessation of sensual pleasures should be understood:
an6.63:14.3: whatever was said in that regard was said because of this.
an6.63:15.1: *Vedanā*, bhikkhus, should be understood … the practice leading to the cessation of *vedanā* should be understood:
an6.63:15.2: this was said. And because of what was it said?
an6.63:16.1: And what, bhikkhus, is the arising from the source of *vedanā*?
an6.63:16.2: Contact, bhikkhus, is the arising from the source of *vedanā*.
an6.63:17.1: And what, bhikkhus, is the diversity of *vedanā*?
an6.63:17.2: There is worldly pleasant *vedanā*, there is unworldly pleasant *vedanā*, there is worldly painful *vedanā*, there is unworldly painful *vedanā*, there is worldly neither-painful-nor-pleasant *vedanā*, there is unworldly neither-painful-nor-pleasant *vedanā*.
an6.63:17.3: This is called the diversity of *vedanā*, bhikkhus.
an6.63:18.1: And what, bhikkhus, is the result of *vedanā*?
an6.63:18.2: When, bhikkhus, feeling, one brings forth this or that embodied existence, either belonging to merit or belonging to demerit,
an6.63:18.3: this is called the result of *vedanā*, bhikkhus.
an6.63:19.1: And what, bhikkhus, is the cessation of *vedanā*?
an6.63:19.2: The cessation of contact, bhikkhus, is the cessation of *vedanā*.
an6.63:19.3: This very Noble Eightfold Path is the practice leading to the cessation of *vedanā*, that is:
an6.63:2.1: “And what, bhikkhus, is that penetrative exposition, that exposition of *dhamma* [*dhammapariyāyaṁ*]?
an6.63:2.2: Sensual pleasures, bhikkhus, should be understood; the arising from the source of sensual pleasures should be understood; the diversity of sensual pleasures should be understood; the result of sensual pleasures should be understood; the cessation of sensual pleasures should be understood; the practice leading to the cessation of sensual pleasures should be understood.
an6.63:20.1: When, bhikkhus, a noble disciple understands *vedanā* in this way, understands the arising from the source of *vedanā* in this way, understands the diversity of *vedanā* in this way, understands the result of *vedanā* in this way, understands the cessation of *vedanā* in this way, understands the practice leading to the cessation of *vedanā* in this way, that one understands this penetrative holy life as the cessation of *vedanā*.
an6.63:20.2: *Vedanā*, bhikkhus, should be understood … the practice leading to the cessation of *vedanā* should be understood:
an6.63:20.3: whatever was said in that regard was said because of this.
an6.63:21.1: *Saññā*, bhikkhus, should be understood … the practice leading to the cessation of *saññā* should be understood:
an6.63:21.2: this was said. And because of what was it said?
an6.63:21.4: *saññā* of forms, *saññā* of sounds, *saññā* of smells, *saññā* of tastes, *saññā* of tangible things, *saññā* of *dhammas*.
an6.63:22.1: And what, bhikkhus, is the arising from the source of *saññā*?
an6.63:22.2: Contact, bhikkhus, is the arising from the source of *saññā*.
an6.63:23.1: And what, bhikkhus, is the diversity of *saññā*?
an6.63:23.2: One *saññā* is in forms, another *saññā* is in sounds, another *saññā* is in smells, another *saññā* is in tastes, another *saññā* is in tangible things, another *saññā* is in *dhammas*.
an6.63:23.3: This is called the diversity of *saññā*, bhikkhus.
an6.63:24.1: And what, bhikkhus, is the result of *saññā*?
an6.63:24.2: I say, bhikkhus, that *saññā* has verbal expression as its result.
an6.63:24.3: However one perceives it, so one speaks of it: ‘Thus I perceived.’
an6.63:24.4: This is called the result of *saññā*, bhikkhus.
an6.63:25.1: And what, bhikkhus, is the cessation of *saññā*?
an6.63:25.2: The cessation of contact, bhikkhus, is the cessation of *saññā*.
an6.63:25.3: This very Noble Eightfold Path is the practice leading to the cessation of *saññā*, that is:
an6.63:26.1: When, bhikkhus, a noble disciple understands *saññā* in this way, understands the arising from the source of *saññā* in this way, understands the diversity of *saññā* in this way, understands the result of *saññā* in this way, understands the cessation of *saññā* in this way, understands the practice leading to the cessation of *saññā* in this way, that one understands this penetrative holy life as the cessation of *saññā*.
an6.63:26.2: *Saññā*, bhikkhus, should be understood … the practice leading to the cessation of *saññā* should be understood.
an6.63:26.3: Whatever was said in that regard was said because of this.
an6.63:27.1: *Āsavas*, bhikkhus, should be understood … the practice leading to the cessation of *āsavas* should be understood:
an6.63:27.2: this was said. And because of what was it said?
an6.63:27.4: the *āsava* of sensuality, the *āsava* of bhava, the *āsava* of blindness [*avijjā*].
an6.63:28.1: And what, bhikkhus, is the arising from the source of *āsavas*?
an6.63:28.2: Blindness [*avijjā*], bhikkhus, is the arising from the source of *āsavas*.
an6.63:29.1: And what, bhikkhus, is the diversity of *āsavas*?
an6.63:29.2: There are *āsavas* leading to hell, there are *āsavas* leading to rebirth in the animal womb, there are *āsavas* leading to the realm of ghosts, there are *āsavas* leading to the human world, there are *āsavas* leading to the world of the *devatās*.
an6.63:29.3: This is called the diversity of *āsavas*, bhikkhus.
an6.63:3.1: *Vedanā*, bhikkhus, should be understood; the arising from the source of *vedanā* should be understood; the diversity of *vedanā* should be understood; the result of *vedanā* should be understood; the cessation of *vedanā* should be understood; the practice leading to the cessation of *vedanā* should be understood.
an6.63:30.1: And what, bhikkhus, is the result of *āsavas*?
an6.63:30.2: When, bhikkhus, one under the sway of blindness [*avijjā*] brings forth this or that embodied existence, either belonging to merit or belonging to demerit, this is called the result of *āsavas*, bhikkhus.
an6.63:31.1: And what, bhikkhus, is the cessation of *āsavas*?
an6.63:31.2: The cessation of blindness [*avijjā*], bhikkhus, is the cessation of *āsavas*.
an6.63:31.3: This very Noble Eightfold Path is the practice leading to the cessation of *āsavas*, that is:
an6.63:32.1: When, bhikkhus, a noble disciple understands *āsavas* in this way, understands the arising from the source of *āsavas* in this way, understands the diversity of *āsavas* in this way, understands the result of *āsavas* in this way, understands the cessation of *āsavas* in this way, understands the practice leading to the cessation of *āsavas* in this way, that one understands this penetrative holy life as the cessation of *āsavas*.
an6.63:32.2: *Āsavas*, bhikkhus, should be understood … the practice leading to the cessation of *āsavas* should be understood.
an6.63:32.3: Whatever was said in that regard was said because of this.
an6.63:33.1: *Kamma*, bhikkhus, should be understood … the practice leading to the cessation of *kamma* should be understood: this was said.
an6.63:33.2: And because of what was it said?
an6.63:33.3: It is intention, bhikkhus, that I call *kamma*.
an6.63:34.1: And what, bhikkhus, is the arising from the source of *kamma*?
an6.63:34.2: Contact, bhikkhus, is the arising from the source of *kamma*.
an6.63:35.1: And what, bhikkhus, is the diversity of *kamma*?
an6.63:35.2: There is *kamma* to be felt in hell, there is *kamma* to be felt in rebirth in the animal womb, there is *kamma* to be felt in the realm of ghosts, there is *kamma* to be felt in the human world, there is *kamma* to be felt in the world of the *devatās*.
an6.63:35.3: This is called the diversity of *kamma*, bhikkhus.
an6.63:36.1: And what, bhikkhus, is the result of *kamma*?
an6.63:36.2: I say, bhikkhus, that the result of *kamma* is threefold—
an6.63:36.3: in this very life, on rebirth, or on some later occasion.
an6.63:36.4: This is called the result of *kamma*, bhikkhus.
an6.63:37.1: And what, bhikkhus, is the cessation of *kamma*?
an6.63:37.2: The cessation of contact, bhikkhus, is the cessation of *kamma*.
an6.63:37.3: This very Noble Eightfold Path is the practice leading to the cessation of *kamma*, that is:
an6.63:38.1: When, bhikkhus, a noble disciple understands *kamma* in this way, understands the arising from the source of *kamma* in this way, understands the diversity of *kamma* in this way, understands the result of *kamma* in this way, understands the cessation of *kamma* in this way, understands the practice leading to the cessation of *kamma* in this way, that one understands this penetrative holy life as the cessation of *kamma*.
an6.63:38.2: *Kamma*, bhikkhus, should be understood … the practice leading to the cessation of *kamma* should be understood:
an6.63:38.3: whatever was said in that regard was said because of this.
an6.63:39.1: *Dukkha*, bhikkhus, should be understood; the arising from the source of *dukkha* should be understood; the diversity of *dukkha* should be understood; the result of *dukkha* should be understood; the cessation of *dukkha* should be understood; the practice leading to the cessation of *dukkha* should be understood.
an6.63:39.2: This was said. And because of what was it said?
an6.63:39.3: Birth too is *dukkha*, aging too is *dukkha*, illness too is *dukkha*, death too is *dukkha*; sorrow, lamentation, pain, distress, and despair too are *dukkha*; not getting what one wants is also *dukkha*; in brief, the five aggregates of clinging/feeding [*upādāna*] are *dukkha*.
an6.63:4.1: *Saññā*, bhikkhus, should be understood; the arising from the source of *saññā* should be understood; the diversity of *saññā* should be understood; the result of *saññā* should be understood; the cessation of *saññā* should be understood; the practice leading to the cessation of *saññā* should be understood.
an6.63:40.1: And what, bhikkhus, is the arising from the source of *dukkha*?
an6.63:40.2: *Taṇhā*, bhikkhus, is the arising from the source of *dukkha*.
an6.63:41.1: And what, bhikkhus, is the diversity of *dukkha*?
an6.63:41.2: There is, bhikkhus, intense *dukkha*; there is slight *dukkha*; there is *dukkha* that fades slowly; there is *dukkha* that fades quickly.
an6.63:41.3: This is called the diversity of *dukkha*, bhikkhus.
an6.63:42.1: And what, bhikkhus, is the result of *dukkha*?
an6.63:42.2: Here, bhikkhus, someone overcome by some *dukkha*, with mind consumed, grieves, grows weary, laments, beats their breast and cries out, and falls into bewilderment. Or else, overcome by some *dukkha*, with mind consumed, they embark on an external search:
an6.63:42.3: ‘Who knows a word or two for the cessation of this *dukkha*?’
an6.63:42.4: I say, bhikkhus, that *dukkha* has bewilderment as its result, or search as its result.
an6.63:42.5: This is called the result of *dukkha*, bhikkhus.
an6.63:43.1: And what, bhikkhus, is the cessation of *dukkha*?
an6.63:43.2: The cessation of *taṇhā*, bhikkhus, is the cessation of *dukkha*.
an6.63:43.3: This very Noble Eightfold Path is the practice leading to the cessation of *dukkha*, that is:
an6.63:44.1: When, bhikkhus, a noble disciple understands *dukkha* in this way, understands the arising from the source of *dukkha* in this way, understands the diversity of *dukkha* in this way, understands the result of *dukkha* in this way, understands the cessation of *dukkha* in this way, understands the practice leading to the cessation of *dukkha* in this way, that one understands this penetrative holy life as the cessation of *dukkha*.
an6.63:44.2: *Dukkha*, bhikkhus, should be understood; the arising from the source of *dukkha* should be understood; the diversity of *dukkha* should be understood; the result of *dukkha* should be understood; the cessation of *dukkha* should be understood; the practice leading to the cessation of *dukkha* should be understood.
an6.63:44.3: Whatever was said in that regard was said because of this.
an6.63:45.1: This, bhikkhus, is that penetrative exposition, that exposition of *dhamma* [*dhammapariyāyaṁ*].”
an6.63:5.1: *Āsavas*, bhikkhus, should be understood; the arising from the source of *āsavas* should be understood; the diversity of *āsavas* should be understood; the result of *āsavas* should be understood; the cessation of *āsavas* should be understood; the practice leading to the cessation of *āsavas* should be understood.
an6.63:6.1: *Kamma*, bhikkhus, should be understood; the arising from the source of *kamma* should be understood; the diversity of *kamma* should be understood; the result of *kamma* should be understood; the cessation of *kamma* should be understood; the practice leading to the cessation of *kamma* should be understood.
an6.63:7.1: *Dukkha*, bhikkhus, should be understood; the arising from the source of *dukkha* should be understood; the diversity of *dukkha* should be understood; the result of *dukkha* should be understood; the cessation of *dukkha* should be understood; the practice leading to the cessation of *dukkha* should be understood.
an6.63:8.1: ‘Sensual pleasures, bhikkhus, should be understood; the arising from the source of sensual pleasures should be understood; the diversity of sensual pleasures should be understood; the result of sensual pleasures should be understood; the cessation of sensual pleasures should be understood; the practice leading to the cessation of sensual pleasures should be understood’: this was said.
an6.63:8.10: A person’s sensual pleasure is passion in intention,
an6.63:8.11: Not these varied things in the world;
an6.63:8.12: A person’s sensual pleasure is passion in intention,
an6.63:8.13: The varied things remain just as they are in the world;
an6.63:8.14: But here the steady ones remove desire.”
an6.63:8.2: And because of what was it said?
an6.63:8.3: These are the five strands of sensual pleasure, bhikkhus—
an6.63:8.4: forms cognizable by the eye, desirable, lovely, agreeable, pleasing, connected with sensuality, enticing,
an6.63:8.8: tangible things cognizable by the body, desirable, lovely, agreeable, pleasing, connected with sensuality, enticing.
an6.63:8.9: But, bhikkhus, these are not called sensual pleasures, these are called strands of sensual pleasure in the noble *vinaya*—
an6.63:9.1: And what, bhikkhus, is the arising from the source of sensual pleasures?
